target.name: Lightmatter
target.founder: Nicholas Harris, Darius Bunandar, Thomas Graham

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $420M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $529M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 25.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: DEEPX
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series C), post-money $529M

data.narrative:
Lightmatter is a Series C semiconductor company that has raised $420M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 8 private peers in chips and semiconductors at similar or adjacent stages—including SambaNova Systems ($5.0B Series D), Tenstorrent ($2.7B Series D), Positron AI ($1.0B Series B), and DEEPX ($529M Series C)—alongside 3 public semiconductor comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD).

Two triangulation methods apply here. Stage-progression analysis, using the $420M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C advancement, yields $714M. Peer-median analysis across the 4-company private comp set produces $1.9B, reflecting the wide dispersion in this sector ($529M–$5.0B range). Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples (25.3x median across the three public tickers) is not applicable given the absence of disclosed revenue for Lightmatter.

The two applicable methods triangulate to a range of $607M (low) to $2.1B (high), with a median of $1.3B. The wide spread reflects both the heterogeneity of stage within the peer set and the early-stage, pre-revenue nature of many frontier semiconductor ventures. Key uncertainties include technology differentiation relative to peers, path to revenue generation, and capital efficiency relative to the $420M already deployed.
